item.title: MCP Security: A Developer’s Guide
item.author: Saurabh Davala
item.content:encoded: Since its release by Anthropic in November 2024, Model Context Protocol (MCP) has gained massive adoption and is quickly becoming the connective tissue between AI agents and the tools, APIs, and data they act on. With just a few lines of configuration, an agent can search code, open tickets, query SaaS systems, or even deploy infrastructure. That kind of flexibility is powerful but it also introduces new security challenges. In fact, security researchers analyzing the MCP ecosystem found command injection flaws affecting 43% of analyzed servers. A single misconfigured or malicious server can exfiltrate secrets, trigger unsafe actions, or quietly change how an agent behaves. This guide is for developers and platform teams building with agents. We’ll unpack what makes MCP workflows uniquely risky for AI infrastructure, highlight common missteps like prompt injection or shadow tooling, and show how secure defaults, like containerized MCP servers and policy-based gateways, can help you govern every tool call without slowing your AI roadmap.Model Context Protocol is a standardized interface that enables AI agents to interact with external tools, databases, and services. MCP security refers to the controls and risks that govern how agents discover, connect to, and execute MCP servers. These security risks span across the entire development lifecycle and involve:: how servers are packaged, signed, versioned, and approved.: how they’re executed on the host vs. in containers, with CPU/memory/network limits.: how calls are mediated, logged, blocked, or transformed in real time.: which tools a given IDE/agent is allowed to see and use.Why does MCP security matter?Securing MCP workflows has become more important than ever because AI agents blur the line between “code” and “runtime.” A prompt or tool description can change what your system is capable of without a code release. This means that security practices have to move up a layer, from static analysis to policy over agent‑tool interactions. Docker codifies that policy in a gateway and makes secure defaults practical for everyday developers.Docker’s approach is to make MCP both and through containerized execution, a policy‑enforcing , and a curated that helps teams standardize what agents can do. If you’re building with agents, this guide will help you understand the risks, why traditional tools fall short, and how Docker reduces blast radius without slowing your AI roadmap.Understanding MCP security risksWhile MCP risks can show up in various ways across the dev lifecycle, there are specific categories they typically fall into. The section below highlights how these risks surface in real workflows, their impact, and practical guardrails that mitigate without slowing teams down. Misconfigurations & weak defaultsRunning servers directly on the host with broad privileges or a persistent state.Unrestricted network egress from tools to the public internet.Unvetted catalogs/registries in client configs, exposing agents to unknown tools. for tool calls-hard to investigate or respond. Lateral movement, data exfiltration, and irreproducible behavior. Always follow MCP server best practices such as leveraging containerization, applying resource and network limits, maintaining an allowlist of approved tools, and capturing call logs centrally.Malicious or compromised servers (supply chain)Typosquatting/poisoned images or unsigned builds. or altered tool metadata that nudges agents into risky actions. Covert behavior change, credential theft, persistent access. Require signature verification, pin versions/digests, and pull from curated sources such as the .Secret management failures in environment variables, prompts, or tool arguments. via tool outputs or model completions. Account takeover, data loss. Use managed secrets, minimize prompt exposure, and redact or block sensitive values at the broker.Prompt injection & tool poisoning: hostile content instructs the model to exfiltrate data or call dangerous tools.: misleading tool descriptions or unexpected defaults that steer the agent. Agents do the wrong thing, confidently. Strict tool allowlists, pre/post‑call interceptors, and output filtering at the gateway. Docker’s MCP Gateway provides active security capabilities (signature checks, call logging, secret and network controls, interceptors).What makes MCP security challenging?Dynamic & non‑deterministic behavior: the same prompt may lead to different tool calls.Instruction vs. data ambiguity: LLMs can treat content (including tool docs) as instructions.Growing, shifting attack surface: every new tool expands what the agent can do instantly.: Static analysis tools don’t see agentic tool calls or MCP semantics; you need mediation between agents and tools, not just better prompts.Implication for developers: You need a guardrail that lives at the agent–tool boundary, verifying what runs, brokering what’s allowed, and recording what happened.How to prevent and mitigate MCP server security concernsUse this practitioner checklist to raise the floor:Containerize every MCP serverRun servers in containers (not on the host) with and a read‑only filesystem where possible. Treat each server as untrusted code with the least privilege necessary.Why it helps limits blast radius and makes behavior reproducible.Centralize enforcement at a gateway (broker)Place a between clients (IDE/agent) and servers. Use it to: Verify before running servers.Maintain a (only approved servers are discoverable).Apply and . requests/responses for audit and incident response.Govern secrets end‑to‑endStore secrets in a managed system; avoid .env files. Prefer short‑lived tokens. Sanitize prompts and tool outputs to reduce exposure.Use (argument/type checks, safety classifiers) and (redaction, PII scrub). Combine with strict tool scoping to reduce prompt‑injection blast radius.Pull servers from curated sources (e.g., ), require signatures, and pin to immutable versions.Alert on anomalous tool sequences (e.g., sudden credential access), and run tabletop exercises to rotate tokens and revoke access.How Docker makes MCP security practicalTurning MCP security from theory into practice means putting guardrails where agents meet tools and making trusted servers easy to adopt for agentic workflows. Docker’s MCP stack does both: Docker Gateway enforces policy and observability on every call, while the Docker MCP Catalog & Toolkit curates, verifies, and versions the servers your team can safely use.Docker MCP Gateway: Your enforcement pointThe gateway sits between clients and servers to provide verification, policy, and observability for every tool call. It supports active security measures like signature verification, call logging, secret and network controls, and pre/post-interceptors so you can block or transform risky actions before they reach your systems. Docker MCP Catalog & Toolkit: Curation and convenienceUse the to standardize the servers your organization trusts. The catalog helps reduce supply‑chain risk (publisher verification, versioning, provenance) and makes it straightforward for developers to pull approved tools into their workflow. With a growing selection of 150+ curated MCP servers, MCP Catalog is a safe and easy way to get started with MCP.Looking for a broader view of how Docker helps with AI development? Check out .Putting it all Together: A practical flowChoose servers from the Catalog and pin them by digest.Register servers with the Gateway so clients only see approved tooling.: verify signatures, log all calls, redact/deny secrets, and restrict egress.Add pre/post interceptors: validate arguments (before), redact/normalize outputs (after).: review call logs, alert on anomalies, rotate secrets, and update allowlists as new tools are introduced.MCP unlocks powerful agentic workflows but also introduces new classes of risk, from prompt injection to tool poisoning and supply‑chain tampering. MCP security isn’t just better prompts; it’s secure packaging, verified distribution, and a brokered runtime with policy.Browse the: Discover 200+ containerized, security-hardened MCP serversDownload the MCP Toolkit in: Get immediate access to secure credential management and container isolation

item.title: Kubernetes v1.34: Decoupled Taint Manager Is Now Stable
item.content:encoded: This enhancement separates the responsibility of managing node lifecycle and pod eviction into two distinct components. Previously, the node lifecycle controller handled both marking nodes as unhealthy with NoExecute taints and evicting pods from them. Now, a dedicated taint eviction controller manages the eviction process, while the node lifecycle controller focuses solely on applying taints. This separation not only improves code organization but also makes it easier to improve taint eviction controller or build custom implementations of the taint based eviction.The feature gate SeparateTaintEvictionController has been promoted to GA in this release. Users can optionally disable taint-based eviction by setting --controllers=-taint-eviction-controller in kube-controller-manager.We offer a huge thank you to all the contributors who helped with design, implementation, and review of this feature and helped move it from beta to stable:Yuan Chen (@yuanchen8911)Aldo Culquicondor (@alculquicondor)Sergey Kanzhelev (@SergeyKanzhelev)Wojciech Tyczynski (@wojtek-t)

item.title: AWS named as a Leader in 2025 Gartner Magic Quadrant for Cloud-Native Application Platforms and Container Management
item.author: Channy Yun (윤석찬)
item.content:encoded: Today, I’m happy to share recent Magic Quadrant reports that named AWS as a Leader in more cloud technology markets: Cloud-Native Application Platforms (aka Cloud Application Platforms) and Container Management.2025 Gartner Magic Quadrant for Cloud-Native Application Platforms AWS has been named a Leader in the Gartner Magic Quadrant for Cloud-Native Application Platforms for 2 consecutive years. AWS was positioned highest on “Ability to Execute”. Gartner defines cloud-native application platforms as those that provide managed application runtime environments for applications and integrated capabilities to manage the lifecycle of an application or application component in the cloud environment.The following image is the graphical representation of the 2025 Magic Quadrant for Cloud-Native Application Platforms.Our comprehensive cloud-native application portfolio—AWS Lambda, AWS App Runner, AWS Amplify, and AWS Elastic Beanstalk—offers flexible options for building modern applications with strong AI capabilities, demonstrated through continued innovation and deep integration across our broader AWS service portfolio.You can simplify the service selection through comprehensive documentation, reference architectures, and prescriptive guidance available in the AWS Solutions Library, along with AI-powered, contextual recommendations from Amazon Q based on your specific requirements. While AWS Lambda is optimized for AWS to provide the best possible serverless experience, it follows industry standards for serverless computing and supports common programming languages and frameworks. You can find all necessary capabilities within AWS, including advanced features for AI/ML, edge computing, and enterprise integration.2025 Gartner Magic Quadrant for Container Management In the 2025 Gartner Magic Quadrant for Container Management, AWS has been named as a Leader for three years and was positioned furthest for “Completeness of Vision”. Gartner defines container management as offerings that support the deployment and operation of containerized workloads. This process involves orchestrating and overseeing the entire lifecycle of containers, covering deployment, scaling, and operations, to ensure their efficient and consistent performance across different environments.The following image is the graphical representation of the 2025 Magic Quadrant for Container Management.AWS container services offer fully managed container orchestration with AWS native solutions and open-source technologies to focus on providing a wide range of deployment options, from Kubernetes to our native orchestrator.You can connect on-premises and edge infrastructure back to AWS container services with EKS Hybrid Nodes and ECS Anywhere, or use EKS Anywhere for a fully disconnected Kubernetes experience supported by AWS. With flexible compute and deployment options, you can reduce operational overhead and focus on innovation and drive business value faster.Gartner does not endorse any vendor, product or service depicted in its research publications and does not advise technology users to select only those vendors with the highest ratings or other designation. Gartner research publications consist of the opinions of Gartner’s research organization and should not be construed as statements of fact. Gartner disclaims all warranties, expressed or implied, with respect to this research, including any warranties of merchantability or fitness for a particular purpose.GARTNER is a registered trademark and service mark of Gartner and Magic Quadrant is a registered trademark of Gartner, Inc. and/or its affiliates in the U.S. and internationally and are used herein with permission. All rights reserved.

item.title: AWS Weekly Roundup: Strands Agents 1M+ downloads, Cloud Club Captain, AI Agent Hackathon, and more (September 15, 2025)
item.author: Channy Yun (윤석찬)
item.content:encoded: Last week, Strands Agents, AWS open source for agentic AI SDK just hit 1 million downloads and earned 3,000+ GitHub Stars less than 4 months since launching as a preview in May 2025. With Strands Agents, you can build production-ready, multi-agent AI systems in a few lines of code.We’ve continuously improved features including support for multi-agent patterns, A2A protocol, and Amazon Bedrock AgentCore. You can use a collection of sample implementations to help you get started with building intelligent agents using Strands Agents. We always welcome your contribution and feedback to our project including bug reports, new features, corrections, or additional documentation.Here is the latest research article of Amazon Science about the future of agentic AI and questions that scientists are asking about agent-to-agent communications, contextual understanding, common sense reasoning, and more. You can understand the technical topic of agentic AI with with relatable examples, including one about our personal behaviors about leaving doors open or closed, locked or unlocked. Here are some launches that got my attention:Amazon EC2 M4 and M4 Pro Mac instances – New M4 Mac instances offer up to 20% better application build performance compared to M2 Mac instances, while M4 Pro Mac instances deliver up to 15% better application build performance compared to M2 Pro Mac instances. These instances are ideal for building and testing applications for Apple platforms such as iOS, macOS, iPadOS, tvOS, watchOS, visionOS, and Safari. LocalStack integration in Visual Studio Code (VS Code) – You can use LocalStack to locally emulate and test your serverless applications using the familiar VS Code interface without switching between tools or managing complex setup, thus simplifying your local serverless development process.AWS CloudTrail MCP Server – New AWS CloudTrail MCP server allows AI assistants to analyze API calls, track user activities, and perform advanced security analysis across your AWS environment through natural language interactions. You can explore more AWS MCP servers for working with AWS service resources.Amazon CloudFront support for IPv6 origins – Your applications can send IPv6 traffic all the way to their origins, allowing them to meet their architectural and regulatory requirements for IPv6 adoption. End-to-end IPv6 support improves network performance for end users connecting over IPv6 networks, and also removes concerns for IPv4 address exhaustion for origin infrastructure. Here are some additional news items that you might find interesting:A city in the palm of your hand – Check out this interactive feature that explains how our AWS Trainium chip designers think like city planners, optimizing every nanometer to move data at near light speed.Measuring the effectiveness of software development tools and practices – Read how Amazon developers that identified specific challenges before adopting AI tools cut costs by 15.9% year-over-year using our cost-to-serve-software framework (CTS-SW). They deployed more frequently and reduced manual interventions by 30.4% by focusing on the right problems first.Become an AWS Cloud Club Captain – Join a growing network of student cloud enthusiasts by becoming an AWS Cloud Club Captain! As a Captain, you’ll get to organize events and building cloud communities while developing leadership skills. Application window is open September 1-28, 2025. Check your calendars and sign up for these upcoming AWS events as well as AWS re:Invent and AWS Summits:AWS AI Agent Global Hackathon – This is your chance to dive deep into our powerful generative AI stack and create something truly awesome. From September 8 to October 20, you have the opportunity to create AI agents using AWS suite of AI services, competing for over $45,000 in prizes and exclusive go-to-market opportunities.AWS Gen AI Lofts – You can learn AWS AI products and services with exclusive sessions and meet industry-leading experts, and have valuable networking opportunities with investors and peers. Register in your nearest city: Mexico City (September 30–October 2), Paris (October 7–21), London (Oct 13–21), and Tel Aviv (November 11–19).That’s all for this week. Check back next Monday for another Weekly Roundup!

item.title: Rails Security in 2025: Automate or Get Breached
item.author: Vaishnavi Ganeshkar
item.content:encoded: When it comes to web applications, speed and features matter, but security is non-negotiable. A single breach can cost more than downtime — it can damage brand trust, trigger compliance penalties, and erode customer confidence.Rails has long been a pioneer in , shipping with built-in protections like CSRF tokens, SQL injection guards, and strong parameter enforcement. But in 2025, with evolving cyber threats, secure defaults aren’t enough — you need active, automated defenses.🔍 Why Rails Security Still Matters in 2025Attackers increasingly use to exploit weak spots faster.Supply chain vulnerabilities (gem dependencies) remain a hidden risk. amplify the damage of small mistakes.Even a simple missed parameter sanitization can open the door to data leaks.⚡ Automating Security with RailsManual audits are no longer practical — security has to be .Brakeman is a static analysis tool built for Rails. It scans your codebase for vulnerabilities like SQL injection, XSS, and unsafe mass assignments.jobs: security_scan: steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v3 - name: Run Brakeman gem install brakemanWith this, every PR automatically gets a , ensuring no risky code slips into production.Use to scan for vulnerable gems.bundle audit check --updateThis helps you patch known CVEs before attackers exploit them.3. Threat Modeling & LogsEnable by default (config.action_dispatch.default_headers).Enforce content security policies (CSP) to prevent injection.Ship logs to a SIEM (Splunk, Datadog, Elastic) to detect unusual request spikes.⚠️ Lessons from Real-World Breaches: Apps that disabled authenticity tokens for convenience later saw unauthorized fund transfers.: Always enforce protect_from_forgery (enabled by default).2. SQL Injection via User Input: A fintech app interpolated strings into queries.: Always use (where("email = ?", input)).: Failing to whitelist strong params allowed users to change their roles.: Always use (params.require(:user).permit(:email, :name)).Each of these could have been prevented by Rails defaults + automated audits.Security isn’t a — it’s an ongoing process. Rails makes it easier with its , but you need to go further:Automate audits with .Continuously monitor dependencies.Review real-world breaches and ensure your app enforces strong parameters, CSPs, and CSRF protections.If you’re running a SaaS or enterprise app on Rails, now is the time to:Add to your CI/CD.Run a today.Review your for potential gaps.🔐 Security isn’t optional — . Build it before attackers test your walls.

item.title: Rails Meets Intelligence: Two Proven Patterns for Adding AI to Your App (OpenAI API + a Python ML…
item.author: Vaishnavi Ganeshkar
item.content:encoded:
Rails Meets Intelligence: Two Proven Patterns for Adding AI to Your App (OpenAI API + a Python ML Microservice)Want AI in your Rails app  — without rewriting your stack? This guide gives you two production-ready patterns: with OpenAI (great for chat assistants, summarization, semantic search/RAG). via a small microservice (great when you need your own models, control, or air-gapped deploys).We’ll build both, wire them into Rails, and show where each shines. Copy-paste friendly, with tests, jobs, and Docker compose.Notes on freshness: we use OpenAI’s (the modern, unified endpoint) and for RAG. Double-check model names and parameters in the official docs when you ship.An service calling OpenAI’s . using OpenAI + Postgres .A that calls your microservice.Background jobs (Sidekiq) for long-running work.You can use each part independently. handles UI, persistence, and orchestration.: text reasoning, summarization, Q&A, and RAG answering.: a small container exposing /predict, loading a scikit-learn pipeline.: store embeddings & run vector search.: background jobs & retries.Postgres 14+ with pgvectorDocker (optional but recommended)Part A — API-based AI in Rails (OpenAI Responses + Embeddings)gem "faraday"gem "faraday_middleware"gem "sidekiq"Postgres extension & column# db/migrate/20240901000000_enable_pgvector.rbclass EnablePgvector < ActiveRecord::Migration[7.1] enable_extension "vector" unless extension_enabled?("vector")end# db/migrate/20240901000001_add_embeddings_to_docs.rbclass AddEmbeddingsToDocs < ActiveRecord::Migration[7.1] add_column :documents, :embedding, :vector, limit: 1536 # choose dims to match your model add_index :documents, :embedding, using: :hnsw, opclass: :vector_cosine_ops endThe pgvector gem provides native vector types and ANN indexes (hnsw, ivfflat), with cosine/inner-product/L2 ops.# .env or credentialsOPENAI_API_KEY=...Reminder: OpenAI docs list the /v1/embeddings). They also show modern model families like text-embedding-3-small/large3) A tiny OpenAI client (Faraday)# app/lib/openai_client.rbclass OpenAIClient def initialize(api_key: ENV.fetch("OPENAI_API_KEY")) @conn = Faraday.new(url: "https://api.openai.com/v1") do |f| f.response :json, content_type: / json$/ f.headers["Authorization"] = "Bearer #{api_key}" f.adapter Faraday.default_adapter end# Responses API (general text/chat) def respond(instructions:, input:, model: "gpt-4o-mini", max_output_tokens: 800) @conn.post("responses") do |req| req.body = { instructions: instructions, # like a system prompt input: input, # string or array of content parts max_output_tokens: max_output_tokens end.body # Embeddings for semantic search / RAG def embed(input:, model: "text-embedding-3-small", dimensions: nil) body = { model: model, input: input } body[:dimensions] = dimensions if dimensions @conn.post("embeddings") { |r| r.body = body }.bodyendinstructions (system-like) and input, with output text available in the response body (client SDKs often expose output_text). Embeddings support an optional dimensions arg for shortening vectors. See docs and model pages for exact fields and current models.4) Seed a tiny knowledge base# app/models/document.rbclass Document < ApplicationRecord # fields: title, body:text, embedding:vector validates :title, :body, presence: truerails g model Document title:string body:textrails db:migrate5) Background job to embed documents# config/initializers/sidekiq.rbRails.application.config.active_job.queue_adapter = :sidekiq# app/jobs/embed_document_job.rbclass EmbedDocumentJob < ApplicationJob doc = Document.find(document_id) text = "#{doc.title} #{doc.body}" openai = OpenAIClient.new vec = openai.embed(input: text)["data"][0]["embedding"] doc.update!(embedding: vec)endSidekiq is a high-performance background job framework for Ruby and integrates easily with Rails. Use it for long calls, retries, and scheduling.When you create or update a document:# app/models/document.rbafter_commit -> { EmbedDocumentJob.perform_later(id) }, on: [:create, :update]6) Vector search (pgvector) + RAG answerer# app/services/semantic_search.rbclass SemanticSearch def initialize(k: 5); @k = k; end openai = OpenAIClient.new qvec = openai.embed(input: query)["data"][0]["embedding"] # cosine distance operator: <-> with vector_cosine_ops index .select("documents.*, (embedding <-> ARRAY[#{qvec.join(',')}]) AS distance") .limit(@k)end# app/services/rag_answerer.rbclass RagAnswerer @openai = OpenAIClient.new top_docs = SemanticSearch.new.nearest(question) context = top_docs.map { |d| "#{d.title}: #{d.body}" }.join(" --- ") resp = @openai.respond( instructions: "You are a helpful, concise Rails expert. Cite facts only from the provided context.", input: [ "QUESTION: #{question}", "Answer with bullet points and include a short sources list using document titles." ], max_output_tokens: 500 # Depending on SDK/response shape; extract text robustly: end def extract_text(resp) # Common pattern: resp["output"] -> array of content parts -> text text = resp.dig("output", 0, "content", 0, "text") endtext-embedding-3-small is a cost-efficient default for search/RAG; you can shorten embedding dimensions if needed.# config/routes.rbresource :assistant, only: [:create]# app/controllers/assistants_controller.rbclass AssistantsController < ApplicationController question = params.require(:question) answer = RagAnswerer.new.call(question: question) render json: { answer: answer }endOpenAI states that, , data sent via the API isn’t used to train their models. Confirm your org’s settings and agreements.Part B — A Python ML Microservice (FastAPI + scikit-learn)This gives you : your own models, offline capability, custom features, and separate scaling.# ml_service/app.pyfrom fastapi import FastAPIfrom pydantic import BaseModelimport joblibmodel = Noneclass PredictIn(BaseModel):class PredictOut(BaseModel): score: float@app.on_event("startup") global model if model is None: model = joblib.load("model.pkl")@app.post("/predict", response_model=PredictOut)def predict(payload: PredictIn): proba = model.predict_proba([payload.text])[0] label = model.classes_[idx] score = float(proba[idx]) return {"label": label, "score": score, "model_version": "1.0.0"}Install & run FastAPI (dev):pip install "fastapi[standard]" scikit-learn joblibfastapi dev ml_service/app.py # auto-reloadThe FastAPI docs show the modern fastapi[standard] extra for a batteries-included dev server and docs at 2) Train a tiny model (toy sentiment)# ml_service/train.pyfrom sklearn.pipeline import Pipelinefrom sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizerfrom sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression "I love this product", "amazing build quality", "works great", "this is awful", "I hate it", "terrible support"]y = ["pos", "pos", "pos", "neg", "neg", "neg"]pipe = Pipeline([ ("vec", TfidfVectorizer(ngram_range=(1,2), min_df=1)), ("clf", LogisticRegression(max_iter=500))pipe.fit(X, y)joblib.dump(pipe, "model.pkl")print("Saved model.pkl"python ml_service/train.py3) Dockerize the microservice# ml_service/DockerfileFROM python:3.11-slimCOPY ml_service/requirements.txt .RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txtCOPY ml_service/ .CMD ["fastapi", "run", "app.py", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]# ml_service/requirements.txtfastapi[standard]joblib# app/services/sentiment_client.rbclass SentimentClient def initialize(base_url: ENV.fetch("ML_SERVICE_URL", "http://ml:8000")) @conn = Faraday.new(url: base_url) do |f| f.response :json, content_type: / json$/ f.adapter Faraday.default_adapter end @conn.post("/predict") { |r| r.body = { text: text } }.bodyend5) Background job + circuit-friendly retries# app/jobs/sentiment_job.rbclass SentimentJob < ApplicationJobrescue_from(StandardError) do |e| retry_job wait: 5.seconds, queue: :low def perform(review_id) review = Review.find(review_id) res = SentimentClient.new.predict(review.body) review.update!(sentiment_label: res["label"], sentiment_score: res["score"]) endDocker Compose (Rails + ML + Postgres + Redis)# docker-compose.ymlservices: image: postgres:15 POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres volumes: ["pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data"] image: redis:7 build: ./ml_service web: command: bash -lc "bundle exec rails s -b 0.0.0.0" DATABASE_URL: postgres://postgres:postgres@db:5432/postgres REDIS_URL: redis://redis:6379/0 OPENAI_API_KEY: ${OPENAI_API_KEY} ML_SERVICE_URL: http://ml:8000 ports: ["3000:3000"] depends_on: ["db", "redis", "ml"] worker: command: bash -lc "bundle exec sidekiq" DATABASE_URL: postgres://postgres:postgres@db:5432/postgres REDIS_URL: redis://redis:6379/0 OPENAI_API_KEY: ${OPENAI_API_KEY} ML_SERVICE_URL: http://ml:8000 depends_on: ["db", "redis", "ml"] pgdata:# spec/services/openai_client_spec.rbrequire "rails_helper"RSpec.describe OpenAIClient do it "embeds text" do stub = stub_request(:post, "https://api.openai.com/v1/embeddings") .to_return(body: { data: [ { embedding: [0.1, 0.2] } ] }.to_json, headers: { "Content-Type" => "application/json" }) out = described_class.new.embed(input: "hello") expect(out["data"][0]["embedding"]).to eq([0.1, 0.2]) expect(stub).to have_been_requested end# tests/test_app.pyfrom fastapi.testclient import TestClientfrom app import app, load_model load_model() res = client.post("/predict", json={"text": "great job"}) assert res.status_code == 200 data = res.json() assert "label" in data and "score" in data: fastest path to useful features (assistants, summarization, reasoning, code help, RAG). Great for launch; you pay per use, no infra for training.: when you need custom features, offline, special models, or strict governance. FastAPI gives you typed I/O, docs at /docs, and easy scaling.: add embeddings + pgvector to ground model answers in your data; cosine distance + ANN indexes keep it snappy.: use Rails credentials / env vars.: set Faraday timeouts; use Sidekiq retries.: log prompt/latency (with redaction), tag job IDs.: cache embeddings and responses when inputs repeat.: rate-limit endpoints; auth your FastAPI service (e.g., shared token header).: confirm vendor data handling for your org.Step-by-step: add this to an existing Rails app (quick path) (faraday, pgvector, sidekiq) and run migrations enabling vector. OpenAIClient and RagAnswerer services. the Document model + embedding job. the FastAPI microservice, train the toy model, dockerize. services (db, redis, ml, web, worker) with environment variables. a controller endpoint (/assistant) and a background SentimentJob. — set monitors, budgets, and alerts.

Ask the knowledge base

<%= form_with url: assistant_path, method: :post, data: { turbo: false } do |f| %> <%= f.text_area :question, rows: 3 %><% end %>

Analyze sentiment

<%= form_with url: reviews_path, method: :post, data: { turbo: false } do |f| %> <%= f.text_area :body, rows: 3 %> <%= f.submit "Classify" %># app/controllers/reviews_controller.rbclass ReviewsController < ApplicationController review = Review.create!(body: params[:body]) SentimentJob.perform_later(review.id) redirect_to root_path, notice: "Queued for classification"endCan I stream Responses to the UI? Yes — use server-sent events or WebSockets; the Responses API supports streaming. Check the official examples for current parameters. Start with text-embedding-3-small for cost/quality, move to -large if needed; consider dimensions to shorten vectors. You can use Sidekiq directly or via Active Job; Sidekiq’s wiki covers best practices, scaling, and retries.Clean, opinionated defaults: keep prompts and extraction in service objects.: always show doc titles in answers; keep the model grounded.: typed inputs/outputs with Pydantic; one endpoint per task; add auth header.Want this tailored to your SaaS?Tell me your domain (CRM, analytics, marketplace, fintech, edtech…) and I’ll adapt:Entities (e.g., , )Prompts + retrieval schemaDocker compose + deploy notes (Fly/Render/K8s)You’ll get ready-to-paste partials and tests wired to your data model.By combining with  — whether through APIs like OpenAI or custom microservices built with FastAPI — you unlock a powerful stack that’s both developer-friendly and production-ready.Rails stays lean, handling user flows, jobs, and UI.Python takes care of heavy ML lifting (training, inference, embeddings).Tools like and make orchestration and retrieval fast at scale.👉 The result? SaaS apps that feel smarter, deliver personalized experiences, and stand out in a crowded market — without rewriting everything in a single tech stack.If you’re building a CRM, marketplace, or analytics SaaS and want to see how AI could be plugged directly into your domain (e.g., smart deal recommendations, predictive pricing, or AI-driven dashboards), let me know — I’ll publish a tailored version with copy-paste partials, jobs, and tests.✨ : Try setting up a small proof-of-concept today — wire Rails to a simple FastAPI service, call OpenAI, and log results in your database. From there, you can grow into full semantic search, recommendation engines, or AI copilots.

item.title: From REST to GraphQL: Supercharging Rails APIs in 2025
item.author: Vaishnavi Ganeshkar
item.content:encoded: In today’s world of and , APIs are the backbone of every user experience. Traditionally, Rails developers leaned on — simple, reliable, and widely adopted. But REST has limitations: over-fetching, under-fetching, and rigid endpoints that don’t scale well across multiple frontends.That’s where GraphQL with Rails (via graphql-ruby) comes in. Instead of hitting multiple REST endpoints, clients can query exactly the data they need — nothing more, nothing less.🔍 Why Move from REST to GraphQL?: One query instead of multiple REST calls.: Mobile, web, and even IoT devices can shape responses differently.: Less payload → faster apps.: A single endpoint that serves many frontend variations.⚡ Implementing GraphQL in a Rails App# Gemfilegem "graphql"gem "graphiql-rails", group: :developmentbundle installrails generate graphql:installThis sets up /graphql as your endpoint and enables in development for interactive queries.2. Define Your First SchemaRails generates a Types folder. Let’s define a simple :# app/graphql/types/post_type.rbmodule Types class PostType < Types::BaseObject field :id, ID, null: false field :title, String, null: false field :content, String, null: true field :author, String, null: false end# app/graphql/types/query_type.rbmodule Types class QueryType < Types::BaseObject field :posts, [Types::PostType], null: false Post.all endAnd get a shaped to your needs.📈 Measuring Performance Gains: To fetch posts and authors, you may hit /posts + /authors.: A single query returns posts .Result: Fewer network calls, smaller payloads, faster render times.🎯 Best Practices for GraphQL in RailsKeep queries — avoid nested queries that become expensive.Use (connections) to handle large data sets.Implement at the field level.Use caching (Apollo, Dataloader) for performance.GraphQL isn’t just hype — it’s becoming the standard for . Rails developers now have the tools to:Serve multiple clients (web, mobile, admin dashboards) from one endpoint.Deliver faster, more flexible user experiences.If your Rails app still runs on , try setting up a this week. Start with one resource (like posts) and measure the difference in API calls, payload size, and response times.🚀 The shift from REST to GraphQL could be the upgrade your SaaS backend needs in 2025.

item.title: Performance & Scalability Enhancements in Rails 8: Faster Apps, Lower Costs
item.author: Vaishnavi Ganeshkar
item.content:encoded: When you’re building a SaaS product, performance isn’t just a developer concern — it’s a business one. Faster response times mean happier users, better retention, and lower cloud bills. With Rails 8, the framework continues to sharpen its edge, focusing on memory efficiency, concurrency, and caching improvements that directly translate to ROI.Let’s break down what’s new, why it matters, and how you can put it into practice.🚀 Rails 8 Performance Upgrades1. ActiveRecord EfficiencyRails 8 introduces smarter query generation and reduced object allocations. By minimizing intermediate objects and cutting down redundant SQL, heavy queries now execute faster with less memory overhead.# Old behaviorUser.where(active: true).select(:id).map(&:id)# New optimized behaviorUser.where(active: true).pluck(:id)This change looks small but can save tens of milliseconds per request when dealing with thousands of records.Rails 8 expands support for (Ruby’s concurrency model) and leverages YJIT improvements in Ruby 3.3, allowing apps to serve more requests per second without extra servers.Result: You can scale (buy fewer servers initially) while handling more throughput.Caching isn’t new, but Rails 8 makes Russian doll caching and fragment caching more efficient by:Reducing cache key collisions.Handling nested template rendering faster.Supporting modern Redis/Memcached backends seamlessly.This matters because the right cache strategy can cut 40–60% of redundant DB hits, directly reducing your cloud costs.4. Parallel Testing ImprovementsTests now run faster by default with parallelization improvements in Rails 8. CI pipelines that used to take 15 minutes can now finish in 6–8.This means your dev team ships features quicker while maintaining quality.📊 Business ROI: Why This Matters for Enterprises: Efficient queries and better caching reduce DB load, saving thousands in AWS/GCP bills.: Every 100ms shaved off page loads improves engagement and conversion rates.: Improved test parallelization speeds up feedback loops, so features ship faster. One Rails SaaS team saw a 25% drop in Postgres costs just by moving from raw .map queries to pluck and introducing Russian doll caching on their dashboards.Rails has always been about , but Rails 8 shows how it’s also about . With faster queries, better concurrency, and smarter caching, you don’t just get snappier apps — you get measurable ROI in reduced infrastructure spend and faster delivery cycles.

item.title: Using Maven Wrapper to Ensure Consistent Maven Versions
item.author: Govinda Solanki
item.content:encoded: When working on a project, it’s important to ensure that everyone on your team is using the same version of Apache Maven. This helps avoid issues that can arise from different Maven versions. One easy way to achieve this is by using the Maven Wrapper.Maven Wrapper is a tool that allows you to specify a particular Maven version for your project. It ensures that anyone who clones your project can build it with the exact same Maven version, without needing to install Maven manually.How to Set Up Maven WrapperTo set up Maven Wrapper for your project, follow these simple steps: and navigate to your project directory.Run the following command to create the Maven Wrapper configuration:mvn wrapper:wrapper -Dmaven="3.9.9"This command generates the necessary files and a mvnw command that will download and run Maven version 3.9.9.Commit the generated files to your version control system (e.g., Git). This ensures that everyone who clones your project will have access to the Maven Wrapper.Once the Maven Wrapper is set up, you can use it to run Maven commands. Instead of using mvn, use ./mvnw (or mvnw.cmd on Windows). For example:This command will use the specified Maven version (3.9.9 in this case) to clean and build your project.Using Maven Wrapper offers several benefits for DevOps teams:: Ensures that all team members and CI/CD pipelines use the same Maven version.: New team members can get started quickly without worrying about installing the correct Maven version.: Simplifies the setup of build environments, making it easier to automate builds and deployments.Did you know that Maven Wrapper can also help you manage different Maven versions for different projects? If you work on multiple projects that require different Maven versions, Maven Wrapper makes it easy to switch between them without any hassle.

item.title: Deploy a PHP web app with Docker & K8s, with NGINX, PHP, and a CI/CD pipeline powered by GitHub…
item.author: Marouane Louguid
item.content:encoded: “Goku” — Simple guide to set up a PHP web app “laravel” — Docker & k8s, and GitHub Actions CI/CDis the standard development environment used to deploy your app locally or in production, with minimal resource usage to optimize your infrastructureThis quick article walks through how we containerized our app with a clean and efficient Docker setup — featuring NGINX, PHP, Redis, and PostgreSQL, all running seamlessly on Containers.This time we decided to use Docker and k8s The Right Way: only one element per container…• Containerized application using PHP, Nginx, PostgreSQL, and Redis• Simple PHP application code for demonstration• CI/CD setup using GitHub Actions• Deployment to Kubernetes (K8s)🧱 The web container — Nginx & Software Collections• NGINX handles the static files and routing. • PHP runs our backend logic. • PostgreSQL takes care of our persistent data. • Redis supports session caching and fast key-value magic.We kept the architecture modular and efficient. Each service is built into its own container with a carefully crafted Dockerfile, making the environment clean, easy to maintain, and ready to scale. Instead of relying on a local-only setup, we build our images and push them to Docker Hub, ensuring consistency across environments. In the next sections, we’ll walk through how each container is built and deployed, how the configuration stays clean, and how everything ties together in our CI/CD pipeline.Let’s dive in. 🚀├── docker-compose.yaml└── app-php/ # your-project│ │ └── index.php│ ├── composer.json│ ├── .env├── database│ ├── Dockerfile│ └── init.sql├── nginx│ ├── Dockerfile│ └── fastcgi_params└── redis ├── .env🚀 To get started quickly, just follow these steps:💡 Before starting the project locally, make sure you have Docker Desktop and Docker Compose installed on your system.Install Docker & Docker Compose on macOSInstall Docker & Docker Compose on WindowsOnce installed, you’re ready to spin up the environment locally.Clone the repository “Goku”git clone https://github.com/mlouguid/Goku.gitNavigate into the project directoryStart the local environment with Docker Composekubernetesk8s│ │ ├── postgres.yaml │ │ ├── php.yaml│ ├── pvc│ │ ├── postgres_pv.yaml │ │ └── postgres_pvc.yaml │ │ └── postgres_secrets.yaml │ ├── postgres_service.yaml │ ├── nginx_service.yaml│ ├── php_service.yaml│ └── redis_service.yamlkubectl apply -R -f kubernetes/k8s🚀 Feel free to open an issue for any problems you encounter happy testing 😁!

item.title: Microservices vs Monolith: A Complete Architecture Guide for Modern Software Development
item.author: Eltac Shixseyidov
item.content:encoded: Microservices vs Monolith: A Complete Architecture Guide for Modern Software DevelopmentIn the ever-evolving landscape of software architecture, one of the most debated topics remains the choice between microservices and monolithic architectures. This decision can make or break your application’s scalability, maintainability, and overall success. Whether you’re a startup founder, a seasoned architect, or a developer preparing for your next interview, understanding these architectural patterns is crucial for making informed decisions.Understanding the FundamentalsWhat is a Monolithic Architecture?A monolithic architecture is a traditional software design pattern where all components of an application are interconnected and deployed as a single unit. Think of it as a large, self-contained building where all rooms are connected through internal corridors — you can’t move one room without affecting the entire structure.Shared database and runtimeInternal method calls for communicationCentralized business logicMicroservices architecture breaks down an application into a collection of loosely coupled, independently deployable services. Each service is responsible for a specific business function and communicates with others through well-defined APIs. Imagine a modern shopping mall where each store operates independently but contributes to the overall shopping experience.Multiple small, independent servicesService-specific databasesNetwork-based communication (REST, gRPC, messaging)The Historical Context: From Monoliths to MicroservicesThe journey from monolithic to microservices architecture didn’t happen overnight. It evolved through several phases:: Everything ran on massive, centralized systems: Introduction of distributed computing conceptsService-Oriented Architecture (SOA): Early attempts at service decomposition: Netflix, Amazon, and other tech giants pioneered the modern approachService-Oriented Architecture (SOA): The Bridge Between Monoliths and MicroservicesBefore diving deeper into microservices vs monoliths, it’s essential to understand SOA, which serves as the conceptual bridge between these two approaches.Service-Oriented Architecture is a design paradigm that structures applications as a collection of services that communicate through well-defined interfaces. SOA predates microservices and laid the groundwork for distributed system thinking.Enterprise Service Bus (ESB) for communicationHeavyweight protocols (SOAP, WS-*)SOA vs Microservices: Key DifferencesAspect SOA Microservices Communication ESB-based, heavyweight protocols Lightweight protocols (REST, messaging) Data Management Shared databases Database per service Governance Centralized Decentralized Service Size Larger, more comprehensive Smaller, focused on single responsibility Technology Stack Standardized across enterprise Technology diversity allowedOther Architecture Types Worth KnowingA hybrid approach that maintains the single deployment unit of a monolith while organizing code into distinct, loosely coupled modules.Medium-sized applicationsTeams that want monolith benefits with better organizationGradual migration path to microservices2. Serverless ArchitectureEvent-driven architecture where code runs in stateless compute containers managed by cloud providers.Function as a Service (FaaS)3. Event-Driven ArchitectureArchitecture pattern where services communicate through events, promoting loose coupling and asynchronous processing.Real-time processing capabilitiesReal-World Scenarios and Use CasesWhen to Choose Monolithic ArchitectureScenario 1: Early-Stage Startup A fintech startup building their first MVP with a small team of 3–5 developers.Faster initial developmentSimpler deployment and debuggingLower operational overheadEasier to iterate and pivotScenario 2: Simple CRUD Application A content management system for a local newspaper.Straightforward business logicLimited scalability requirementsCost-effective maintenanceWhen to Choose Microservices ArchitectureScenario 1: E-commerce Platform (Amazon-scale) A large e-commerce platform serving millions of users globally.Different scaling requirements for catalog, payment, inventoryMultiple teams working independentlyNeed for technology diversityHigh availability requirementsScenario 2: Streaming Service (Netflix-like) A video streaming platform with complex recommendation algorithms.User management, content delivery, recommendations are distinct domainsDifferent performance characteristicsNeed for rapid feature deploymentGlobal scale requirementsDetailed Comparison: Monoliths vs MicroservicesDevelopment and Deployment: Single codebase, shared development environment: Easier integration testing, but longer test cycles: Single deployment unit, all-or-nothing updates: Simple but affects entire applicationMicroservices Architecture:: Multiple codebases, independent development cycles: Complex integration testing, faster unit testing: Independent service deployment, gradual rollouts possible: Granular rollbacks, but dependency management complexityPerformance and ScalabilityLower latency for internal communicationsShared resources can lead to performance bottlenecksVertical scaling limitationsResource utilization can be inefficientMicroservices Performance:Network latency between servicesIndependent scaling of bottleneck servicesBetter resource utilizationHorizontal scaling advantagesMonolithic Data Approach:Single database, ACID transactionsPotential for database bottlenecksMicroservices Data Approach:Database per service principleEventual consistency challengesData synchronization complexityBetter data isolation and securityTeam Structure and Conway’s LawConway’s Law states: “Organizations design systems that mirror their own communication structure.”Monolithic Team Structure:Single team or closely coordinated teamsShared responsibility for entire codebaseCentralized decision makingMicroservices Team Structure:Cross-functional teams owning specific servicesIndependent decision making within service boundariesClear ownership and accountabilityRequires mature DevOps practicesCommon Interview Questions and AnswersQuestion 1: “When would you choose a monolith over microservices?” “I would choose a monolith when:The team size is small (under 10 developers)The application domain is well-understood and unlikely to change dramaticallyTime-to-market is critical for an MVPThe operational complexity of distributed systems isn’t justified by the business requirementsThe organization lacks mature DevOps practicesFor example, a startup building their first product would benefit from a monolith’s simplicity, allowing them to iterate quickly and validate their business model before considering the complexity of microservices.”Question 2: “How do you handle data consistency in microservices?” “Data consistency in microservices requires a shift from ACID transactions to eventual consistency patterns:: Coordinate transactions across services using choreography or orchestration: Store events that represent state changes, allowing reconstruction of current state: Separate read and write models for better scalability and consistency: Implement rollback logic for failed distributed transactionsThe key is to design services with proper boundaries so that most operations can be completed within a single service, minimizing the need for distributed transactions.”Question 3: “What are the main challenges of microservices architecture?” “The main challenges include:Distributed System Complexity: Network failures, latency, debugging across services: Eventual consistency, distributed transactions, data synchronization: API versioning, service discovery, circuit breakersMonitoring and Observability: Distributed tracing, log aggregation, metrics collection: Container orchestration, CI/CD pipelines, infrastructure management: Integration testing, contract testing, end-to-end testing complexity: Service-to-service authentication, network security, data protection”Question 4: “How do you decompose a monolith into microservices?” “I follow a strategic approach:: Identify bounded contexts and business capabilities: Gradually replace monolith components with microservices: Start with read replicas, then separate write databases: Introduce to manage service routing and cross-cutting concernsExtract Services Gradually: Begin with least dependent, most business-critical services: Plan for data consistency during transition: Implement comprehensive testing at each stepThe key is to do this incrementally, ensuring system stability throughout the migration.”Question 5: “How do microservices handle communication failures?” “Microservices must be designed for failure:: Prevent cascading failures by stopping calls to unhealthy servicesRetry with Exponential Backoff: Handle transient failures gracefully: Set appropriate timeouts to prevent hanging operations: Isolate resources to prevent total system failure: Provide degraded functionality when dependencies fail: Monitor service health and remove unhealthy instancesAsynchronous Communication: Use messaging for non-critical operations to reduce coupling”Migration Strategies: Moving Between ArchitecturesMonolith to Microservices MigrationThe Strangler Fig Pattern:Identify service boundaries using domain-driven designCreate new microservice alongside monolithRoute new features to microserviceGradually migrate existing functionalityRemove code from monolith once migration is completeDatabase Decomposition Strategy:Start with shared databaseCreate service-specific read replicasImplement data synchronization mechanismsFinally separate databases completelyMicroservices to Monolith MigrationSometimes, teams realize they’ve over-engineered their solution. The reverse migration involves:Identifying service boundaries that don’t align with business needsConsolidating related servicesMerging databases where appropriateSimplifying deployment and monitoringTechnology Stack ConsiderationsMonolithic Technology Choices: Java, C#, Python, Ruby, Node.js : Spring Boot, ASP.NET Core, Django, Rails, Express : PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server : Traditional servers, containers, cloud platformsMicroservices Technology Ecosystem: Kong, Ambassador, AWS API Gateway, Azure API Management : Istio, Linkerd, Consul Connect : Kubernetes, Docker Swarm, AWS ECS : Apache Kafka, RabbitMQ, AWS SQS, Azure Service Bus : Prometheus, Grafana, Jaeger, Zipkin, ELK Stack : Consul, Eureka, Kubernetes DNSPerformance and Monitoring ConsiderationsApplication Performance Monitoring (APM) toolsDatabase performance monitoringServer resource monitoringSingle point of logging and metrics: Track requests across multiple services Service Mesh Observability: Monitor service-to-service communication : Aggregate logs from all services : Monitor individual service health : Define and monitor service level objectivesSingle authentication pointCentralized authorizationService-to-service authentication (mTLS)Distributed authorizationSecrets management across servicesTotal Cost of Ownership (TCO)Lower development complexitySimpler infrastructure requirementsReduced operational overheadFewer specialized skills neededHigher infrastructure costs (container orchestration, service mesh)Increased operational complexityNeed for specialized DevOps skillsMore sophisticated monitoring and toolingWhen the Investment Pays OffMicroservices typically provide ROI when:Application serves millions of usersMultiple teams need to work independentlyDifferent parts of the system have vastly different scaling requirementsTechnology diversity provides competitive advantagesMy Considerations and RecommendationsAfter working with both architectures across various projects and scales, here are my practical recommendations:Start with a Modular MonolithFor most projects, I recommend starting with a well-structured modular monolith. This approach provides:Clear service boundaries from day oneAbility to migrate to microservices when neededLower complexity during initial developmentBetter understanding of domain boundaries before committing to distributed architectureThe “Microservices Readiness” ChecklistBefore moving to microservices, ensure your organization has:[ ] Mature CI/CD practices[ ] Container orchestration expertise[ ] Distributed systems monitoring capabilities[ ] Multiple teams that would benefit from independent deployment[ ] Clear understanding of service boundaries[ ] DevOps culture and practicesThe Sweet Spot: Hybrid ApproachesIn practice, the most successful architectures often combine elements of both:Core business logic in microservicesSupporting functionality in modular componentsShared infrastructure servicesEvent-driven communication where appropriateTechnology-First vs Domain-First ThinkingAvoid the trap of choosing architecture based solely on technology trends. Instead:Start with business requirementsConsider team structure and capabilitiesEvaluate scalability needsAssess operational maturityThen choose the appropriate architectureThink of architecture as evolutionary, not revolutionary:Phase 1: Well-structured monolith with clear module boundariesPhase 2: Extract high-value, independent servicesPhase 3: Gradually decompose based on business needs and scaling requirementsPhase 4: Mature microservices ecosystem with full observabilityThe choice between microservices and monolithic architecture isn’t binary — it’s a spectrum of trade-offs that depend on your specific context, team capabilities, and business requirements. While microservices offer compelling benefits for large-scale, complex systems, monoliths remain an excellent choice for many applications.The key to success lies not in following architectural trends, but in understanding your requirements, constraints, and team capabilities. Start simple, build incrementally, and evolve your architecture as your understanding of the domain and scaling requirements becomes clearer.Remember: the best architecture is the one that enables your team to deliver value to customers efficiently while maintaining system reliability and developer productivity. Sometimes that’s a well-crafted monolith, sometimes it’s a sophisticated microservices ecosystem, and often it’s something in between.Whether you’re building the next unicorn startup or modernizing enterprise systems, the principles outlined in this guide will help you make informed architectural decisions that serve your business goals both today and in the future.

item.title: Hexagonal Architecture in Java: A Guide to Ports & Adapters with Spring Boot
item.author: Rishi
item.content:encoded: Hexagonal Architecture, also known as Ports & Adapters, represents a significant evolution from traditional layered architectures in Java…
